warning: AI-assisted monitoring. Verify against official legislation before product approval, export, label declaration, or customer compliance confirmation.

rules.sourceTitle: Standard for Food Additive Use - GB2760-2015
rules.notes: The provided text for this URL was a raw PDF stream and could not be parsed to extract specific limits. This rule is inferred from the document title, indicating the existence of a standard for food additive use.
rules.evidenceQuote: Standard for Food Additive Use - GB2760-2015

rules.limit: Verification required via GSFA Online Database
rules.sourceTitle: Food Additives DB (GSFA Online) | CODEXALIMENTARIUS FAO-WHO
rules.notes: The Codex General Standard for Food Additives (GSFA, Codex STAN 192-1995) sets forth the conditions under which permitted food additives may be used in all foods. Users are encouraged to consult the database and its Preamble for specific provisions.
rules.evidenceQuote: The "Codex General Standard for Food Additives" (GSFA, Codex STAN 192-1995) sets forth the conditions under which permitted food additives may be used in all foods...

rules.parameter: Contaminants (e.g., mycotoxins, metals like arsenic, lead, mercury)
rules.limit: Verification required via Codex General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995)
rules.sourceTitle: Contaminants | CODEXALIMENTARIUS FAO-WHO
rules.notes: Codex establishes Maximum Levels (MLs) for contaminants in food and feed, which are the maximum concentrations legally permitted to protect human health. The Codex Committee on Contaminants in Food (CCCF) establishes and endorses these levels.
rules.evidenceQuote: The Codex maximum level (ML) for a contaminant in a food or feed commodity is the maximum concentration of that substance recommended by the Codex Alimentarius Commission to be legally permitted in that commodity.

rules.limit: Follow Code of Practice for the Prevention and Reduction of Mycotoxins in Spices (CXC 78-2017)
rules.sourceTitle: Contaminants | CODEXALIMENTARIUS FAO-WHO
rules.notes: A specific Code of Practice is available for the prevention and reduction of mycotoxin contamination in spices.
rules.evidenceQuote: CXC 78-2017 Code of Practice for the Prevention and Reduction of Mycotoxins in Spices

rules.sourceTitle: Database - Food Safety - European Commission
rules.notes: The database serves as a tool to inform about food additives approved for use in the EU and their conditions of use, based on Annex II of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008.
rules.evidenceQuote: This database can serve as a tool to inform about the food additives approved for use in food in the EU and their conditions of use. It is based on the Union list of food additives. This list is in Annex II of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008.

rules.parameter: Foodborne bacteria, toxins, and metabolites (e.g., Salmonella, Listeria monocytogenes, Enterobacter sakazakii, staphylococcal enterotoxins, histamine)
rules.sourceTitle: Microbiological criteria - Food Safety - European Commission
rules.notes: Commission Regulation (EC) No 2073/2005 lays down food safety criteria for relevant foodborne bacteria, their toxins and metabolites, and process hygiene criteria for foods placed on the market.
rules.evidenceQuote: Commission Regulation (EC) No 2073/2005 on microbiological criteria for foods, applicable from 1 January 2006, lays down food safety criteria for relevant foodborne bacteria, their toxins and metabolites, such as Salmonella , Listeria monocytogenes , Enterobacter sakazakii , staphylococcal enterotoxins and histamine in specific foods.

rules.sourceTitle: GSO 9:2022 Gulf Technical Regulation - Labeling of prepackaged food stuffs
rules.notes: This regulation sets out the general requirements for labeling of prepackaged food products.
rules.evidenceQuote: GSO 9:2022 لائحة فنية خليجية --> بطاقات المواد الغذائية المعبأة

rules.foodCategory: Foodstuffs intended for human consumption and food ingredients
rules.sourceTitle: GSO 1016:2015 Gulf Technical Regulation - Microbiological Criteria for Foodstuffs
rules.notes: This regulation specifies microbiological limits for various foodstuffs and food ingredients used in the food industry.
rules.evidenceQuote: This GSO technical regulation is concerned with microbiological limits for some foodstuffs intended for human consumption and for some food ingredients used in food industry.

rules.sourceTitle: Compendium of Food Additives Regulations
rules.notes: Specific additive limits and permitted uses must be verified against the full regulation document.
rules.evidenceQuote: Compendium_Food_Additives_Regulations

rules.sourceTitle: Compendium of Labelling and Display Regulations

rules.notes: Specific chemical contaminant limits for processed foods, including seasoning and snack products, are contained within the regulations listed under 'Pangan Olahan'.

rules.notes: General labelling requirements, including warnings, for processed foods, including seasoning and snack products, are contained within the regulations listed under 'Pangan Olahan'.

rules.notes: Specific microbiological limits for processed foods, including seasoning and snack products, are contained within the regulations listed under 'Pangan Olahan'.

rules.sourceTitle: Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare: Food Safety Information
rules.notes: Compliance with regulations concerning food additives is required.
rules.evidenceQuote: Food Additives

rules.sourceTitle: Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare: Food Safety Information
rules.notes: Compliance with the Positive List System for Agricultural Chemical Residues in Food is required.
rules.evidenceQuote: Positive List System for Agricultural Chemical Residues in Food

rules.sourceTitle: Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare: Food Safety Information
rules.notes: Compliance with the 'Specifications and Standards for Food, Food Additives, etc. Under The Food Sanitation Act' is required for various aspects of food safety and composition.
rules.evidenceQuote: Specifications and Standards for Food, Food Additives, etc. Under The Food Sanitation Act

rules.parameter: Nutrition Labeling, Health Claims, Special Dietary Uses
rules.sourceTitle: Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare: Food Safety Information
rules.notes: Compliance with regulations for food with health claims, food for special dietary uses, and nutrition labeling is required.
rules.evidenceQuote: Food with Health Claims, Food for Special Dietary Uses, and Nutrition Labeling

rules.parameter: General labelling requirements for prepackaged food
rules.sourceTitle: GSO 9:2022 Technical Regulation - Labels of Prepackaged Food
rules.notes: This regulation sets out the general requirements for labelling of prepackaged food products in the GCC region, which includes seasoning and snack products.
rules.evidenceQuote: بطاقات المواد الغذائية المعبأة

rules.limit: Verification required as limits vary by additive and food type
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.evidenceQuote: Different limits apply, depending on the additive and the type of food products. Use the Guidance Information on Requirements for Use of Food Additives to learn more about the different types of additives and the corresponding regulations that impose the limits.

rules.limit: Must be assessed for safety by SFA
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.evidenceQuote: Food additives must be assessed for safety by SFA before they can be used in food sold in Singapore.

rules.limit: Only SFA-assessed additives permitted for use
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.evidenceQuote: Only food additives that have been assessed by SFA are permitted for use in food products sold in Singapore.

rules.limit: Must have technological justification and not present health risks to consumers
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.evidenceQuote: Food additives are allowed when: there is a technological justification they do not present health risks to consumers

rules.limit: Use Calculation Tool for Class II Chemical Preservatives to determine permitted levels
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.evidenceQuote: If two or more Class II chemical preservatives are to be used in the food (e.g. sorbic acid or benzoic acid), use the Calculation Tool for Class II Chemical Preservatives to calculate the permitted levels that should be used.

rules.limit: Do not import or sell food products containing non-permitted additives
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.evidenceQuote: If your food product contains an additive that is not permitted, do not import or sell it.

rules.limit: Verification required using SFA's List of Permitted Food Additives or Food Additives Search tool
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.evidenceQuote: Download the List of Permitted Food Additives or use the Food Additives Search tool to check if your additives are allowed.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.evidenceQuote: All food businesses offering food for sale in Singapore must comply with the regulatory limits on food additives.

rules.limit: Food businesses must be aware and conduct laboratory tests as needed
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.evidenceQuote: Food businesses must be aware of the contaminants that are commonly found in their food products and conduct laboratory tests as needed.

rules.limit: Must be within SFA’s regulatory limits
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.evidenceQuote: Contaminants (also known as incidental constituents) in food sold in Singapore must be within SFA’s regulatory limits.

rules.limit: Do not import or sell food products containing contaminants exceeding regulatory limits
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.evidenceQuote: If your food or food products contain contaminants or incidental constituents that exceed the regulatory limits, do not import or sell them.

rules.foodCategory: Meat and any food derived from meat
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.evidenceQuote: Regulation 33 of the Food Regulations prohibits the import or sale of meat and any food derived from meat which contains residues of the following compounds: diethylstibestrol [...] hexoestrol [...] dienoestrol

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.evidenceQuote: All food businesses must comply with the regulatory limits on incidental constituents i.e. contaminants in food.

rules.parameter: Various contaminants (microbiological, heavy metals, mycotoxins, marine biotoxins, veterinary drug residues, pesticide residues, other incidental constituents)
rules.limit: Verification of specific limits required from relevant SFA documents
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.evidenceQuote: There are various limits imposed on different types of contaminants or incidental constituents in food. For more details, please refer to the relevant documents below: Microbiological standards [...] Heavy metals [...] Mycotoxins and Marine biotoxins [...] Veterinary drug residues [...] Pesticide residues [...] Other incidental constituents

rules.limit: Verification of Maximum Residue Limits (MRLs) required
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.evidenceQuote: SFA has also assessed the risk of the presence of other veterinary drug residues in various food commodities, and has established a list of maximum residue limits (MRLs) for veterinary drug residues for compliance by food businesses with effect from 1 May 2020.

rules.limit: Must be declared in list of ingredients on food labels
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.evidenceQuote: All ingredients and additives used in food products must be declared in list of ingredients on food labels.

rules.limit: Food products must comply with labelling requirements
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.evidenceQuote: Your food or food product must also comply with the labelling requirements.

rules.limit: Verification required from relevant SFA documents
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.evidenceQuote: Microbiological standards Ready-to-eat food Non-ready-to-eat food (till 3 Mar 2024) Non-ready-to-eat food (from 4 Mar 2024)

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.evidenceQuote: Ready-to-eat (RTE) food are not required to be tested for total plate count (TPC).

rules.foodCategory: Prepackaged foods, Processed foods, Seasoning, Snack food

rules.notes: Recommended labeling for these allergens when present as an ingredient or food additive.

rules.foodCategory: Prepackaged foods, Processed foods, Seasoning, Snack food

rules.notes: Mandatory labeling for these allergens when present as an ingredient or food additive.
rules.evidenceQuote: food allergen labeling is required for the products that contain any of the following 11 allergens as an ingredient or food additive

rules.foodCategory: Prepackaged foods, Processed foods, Seasoning, Snack food

rules.notes: Mandatory labeling for sulphite if the residue sulphur dioxide is 10 mg/kg or more.
rules.evidenceQuote: sulphite as the residue sulphur dioxide for 10 or more milligrams per kilogram of food.

rules.foodCategory: Food products, Processed foods, Seasoning, Snack food
rules.sourceTitle: Regulations Governing Food Allergen Labeling on the Recommended Labeling Allergens
rules.notes: Suggested statement for products with a risk of allergen cross-contamination.
rules.evidenceQuote: the following statement of precautionary allergen labeling is suggested: “ The plant, equipment or production line used to produce the product is also used to process○○” or the synonymous terms.

rules.foodCategory: Prepackaged foods, Processed foods, Seasoning, Snack food
rules.sourceTitle: Revised Regulations on Nutrition Claim for Prepackaged Food Products

rules.limit: Verification required against FDA regulations and inventories
rules.sourceTitle: Food Additive Listings | FDA, Substances Added to Food (formerly EAFUS) | FDA
rules.notes: Includes substances listed in 21 CFR Parts 172, 173, 73, 74, 182, 184, 181, and the Substances Added to Food inventory.
rules.evidenceQuote: Food additives and color additives that are listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Parts 172, 173 and Parts 73, 74 respectively), and flavoring substances evaluated by FEMA* and JECFA*. Generally Recognized as Safe ("GRAS") substances that are listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Parts 182 and 184). Substances approved for specific uses in foods prior to September 6, 1958, known as prior-sanctioned substances (21 CFR Part 181).

rules.sourceTitle: Substances Added to Food (formerly EAFUS) | FDA
rules.notes: Listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Part 189) as 'PROHIBITED' or 'PROHIBITED WITH EXCEPTIONS'.
rules.evidenceQuote: Prohibited substances that are listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Part 189) as prohibited from use in food (labeled as "PROHIBITED" or "PROHIBITED WITH EXCEPTIONS").

rules.limit: milk, eggs, fish, Crustacean shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soybeans, and sesame
rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: These 9 allergens are identified by law (FALCPA and FASTER Act). Sesame was added effective January 1, 2023.
rules.evidenceQuote: The major food allergens are milk, eggs, fish, Crustacean shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soybeans, and sesame. The change was effective on January 1, 2023.

rules.sourceTitle: Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool
rules.notes: This is a Guidance Level for Acid-hydrolyzed protein and Asian-style sauces.
rules.evidenceQuote: 3-Monochloropropane-1,2-Diol (3-MCPD) Acid-hydrolyzed protein and Asian-style sauces Guidance Level 1 ppm CPG 500.500

rules.sourceTitle: Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool
rules.notes: This is an Action Level. Applies to general foods, and specifically peanuts and peanut products.
rules.evidenceQuote: Aflatoxins, total Foods Action Level 20 ppb CPG 555.400; Aflatoxins, total Peanuts and peanut products Action Level 20 ppb CPG 570.375

rules.parameter: Chemical contaminants (tolerances, action levels, guidance levels, etc.)
rules.limit: Verification required against the Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool and 21 CFR parts 109 and 509
rules.sourceTitle: Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool
rules.notes: The tool provides a consolidated list of levels for poisonous or deleterious substances. Last updated on March 20, 2025.
rules.evidenceQuote: The Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool provides a consolidated list of these levels. Contaminant levels listed in the transparency tool include tolerances, action levels, guidance levels, derived intervention levels, recommended maximum levels, and advisory levels. Tolerances and action levels are defined in 21 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) parts 109 and 509.

rules.limit: Must meet defined standard for gluten content
rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: FDA issued a final rule defining 'gluten-free' for food labeling and compliance requirements for fermented and hydrolyzed foods.
rules.evidenceQuote: On August 2, 2013, the FDA issued a final rule defining “gluten-free” for food labeling... On August 12, 2020, the FDA issued a final rule to establish compliance requirements for fermented and hydrolyzed foods, or foods that contain fermented or hydrolyzed ingredients, bearing the “gluten-free” claim.

rules.limit: Should not be used as a substitute for adhering to current good manufacturing practices
rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: Such statements are not required by law and can be used to address unavoidable 'cross-contact' only if manufacturers have incorporated good manufacturing processes.
rules.evidenceQuote: Such statements are not required by law and can be used to address unavoidable “cross-contact,” only if manufacturers have incorporated good manufacturing processes in their facility and have taken every precaution to avoid cross-contact... FDA guidance and regulations for the food industry states that advisory statements should not be used as a substitute for adhering to current good manufacturing practices and must be truthful and not misleading.

rules.limit: Required to prevent cross-contact and undeclared allergens
rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: Established by the 'Current Good Manufacturing Practice, Hazard Analysis, and Risk-Based Preventive Controls for Human Food' rule (21 CFR part 117). Facilities must have written procedures.
rules.evidenceQuote: The FDA’s “Current Good Manufacturing Practice, Hazard Analysis, and Risk-Based Preventive Controls for Human Food” rule (CGMP & PC rule, 21 CFR part 117) establishes requirements applicable to establishments that manufacture, process, pack, or hold human food. The CGMP & PC rule includes requirements for allergen preventive controls to prevent allergen cross-contact in manufacturing and packaging and to prevent undeclared allergens.

rules.limit: Must be declared on the label
rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: Identified as allergenic substances derived from insects.
rules.evidenceQuote: Color additives made from cochineal extract and carmine, which are derived from insects, have been identified as allergenic substances that must be declared on the label of all food and cosmetic products.

rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: The food source of a major food allergen must appear in parentheses following the ingredient name OR immediately after or next to the ingredient list in a 'contains' statement. Specific types of tree nuts, fish, and Crustacean shellfish must be declared.
rules.evidenceQuote: FALCPA requires that foods or ingredients that contain a "major food allergen" be specifically labeled with the name of the allergen source. The allergen's food source must be declared at least once on the food label in one of two ways. The name of the food source of a major food allergen must appear: In parentheses following the name of the ingredient... — OR — Immediately after or next to the list of ingredients in a “contains” statement.

rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: Required due to potential hypersensitivity reactions in some individuals.
rules.evidenceQuote: The FDA requires all products containing FD&C Yellow No. 5 to identify it on their labels so consumers who are sensitive to the dye can avoid it.

rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies | FDA
rules.notes: Must be declared on food labels when present at or above the specified concentration due to potential adverse reactions.
rules.evidenceQuote: Various sulfiting agents, including sodium bisulfite, are allowed as food ingredients. But due in part to adverse reactions to them, such as asthma in sensitive individuals, they must be declared on food labels when present in food and the concentration in the food is ≥10 parts per million total sulfites.
